participants.reasoning: The extracted participants description matches the official study details overall, including sample size after screening, demographics, setting, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and baseline cocaine use being in the same general range.
participants.extracted: Ninety-three cocaine- and opioid co-dependent patients were enrolled and stabilized on methadone during a 2-week screening period at Yale University (n=40) and Baylor College of Medicine (n=53). The final genotyped sample with at least one cocaine-positive urine was 67 participants, and the primary urine toxicology analyses included 56 participants with complete 12-week data. Opioid and/or cocaine dependence was diagnosed by interview using DSM-IV criteria. Participants were assessed with the MINI and ASI at baseline. Exclusion criteria included current other drug or alcohol dependence (except tobacco), unstable major medical illness, history of major psychiatric disorder (psychosis, schizophrenia, bipolar disorder), current suicidality, and inability to read and understand consent. Women of childbearing potential required negative pregnancy testing, contraception, and monthly pregnancy tests. The sample was mostly male (71%) and Caucasian (76%), with a mean age of 38 years; about half had prior methadone maintenance treatment and average cocaine use was 12 years, with use on 17 days in the month before study entry.
participants.official:
Number randomised:74
Sex (% male):64.5%
Age:mean 38.75 years
Ethic background:75.7 % "Caucasian" (understood to mean White)
Setting/country:outpatient, USA
Civil status:not reported
Employment status:59.5% unemployed
Baseline cocaine use:19 days in past month
Method of cocaine use:not reported
Baseline alcohol use:39% reported history of alcohol abuse
Inclusion criteria
DSM‐IV criteria for opioid and cocaine dependence and ≥ 1 cocaine‐positive urine sample during the 2‐week period with methadone

interventions.reasoning: The extracted text matches the official intervention details, including disulfiram/placebo dosing, methadone maintenance at 60 mg/day, CBT, and the 12-week duration.
interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized to receive either disulfiram 250 mg daily or placebo for 12 weeks. All patients also received weekly individual cognitive behavioral therapy and were maintained on methadone treatment, which was increased from an initial 25 mg/day by 5 mg/day to a maintenance dose of 60 mg/day.
interventions.official:
EG:disulfiram (250 mg/day) plus methadone (60 mg/day) and CBT (n = 34)
CG:placebo plus methadone (60 mg/day) and CBT (n = 40)
Duration of intervention:12 weeks (2 weeks with methadone and 10 weeks with disulfiram/placebo)

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes describe urine-based cocaine use over a 12-week treatment period and mention no explicit treatment adherence, daily crack dose, drug-free rate, or side effects outcome as specified in the official record.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure cocaine use outcomes as the percentage of cocaine-positive urine samples (benzoylecgonine, urine toxicology) collected three times weekly over the 12-week treatment period, with analyses focused on change over time across baseline and follow-up intervals (reported in 2-week periods, including the last 2 weeks of treatment) and stratified by treatment group and MTHFR genotype. Acceptability of treatment was not described as a separate planned outcome in the provided text, and no explicit secondary safety scale or adverse-event measure was specified beyond routine clinical monitoring during the 12-week study.
outcomes.official:
Treatment adherence (number of subjects who received treatment for the full 60 days)
Frequency of drug use (mean of number of days per week that crack cocaine was used)
Drug dose (daily mean dose of crack cocaine used; in grams)
Drug‐free rate (number of subjects that stopped crack cocaine use after 60 days, confirmed by urinalysis)
Side effects
